primary_diagnosis.name: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, suspected early acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) post-aspiration

pathophysiology_summary: A witnessed aspiration event approximately 36 hours prior has triggered a severe local and systemic inflammatory response, causing diffuse alveolar damage and increased capillary permeability — non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, most consistent with early ARDS. Unlike cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (which is driven by elevated hydrostatic pressure from left ventricular failure and classically improves with upright positioning and diuresis), this process floods the alveoli with protein-rich fluid due to a damaged alveolar-capillary membrane, and severity is not relieved by positional change — a key distinguishing historical feature obtainable via telehealth. The absence of any prior heart failure history and the clear temporal relationship to the aspiration event both argue strongly against a cardiac cause. Profound, refractory hypoxaemia (SpO2 84% despite a well-ventilated room) with mental sluggishness indicates severe respiratory compromise approaching the threshold for advanced airway management and mechanical ventilation. Via telehealth, auscultation, JVP assessment, and chest imaging cannot be obtained, but the reported lack of positional relief, clear aspiration precedent, absence of cardiac history, and profound refractory hypoxaemia/exhaustion are sufficient to mandate immediate intensive-care-paramedic-level ambulance dispatch.

differential.evidence_basis: Severe refractory dyspnoea following a recent significant aspiration event.
Must be considered in any patient with acute severe dyspnoea, particularly an older patient, though the lack of positional relief and absence of prior cardiac history argue against it.

A recognised complication of aspiration; the recent completed antibiotic course and rapid, severe, bilateral deterioration make an inflammatory (ARDS) rather than purely infective process more likely, though co-existing infection cannot be excluded.
Should always be screened for in acute severe dyspnoea, though the clear temporal link to aspiration and diffuse (rather than focal) process make this less likely as the primary explanation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The clear aspiration precedent and severity should place non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema/ARDS as leading from the opening complaint, with cardiogenic oedema retained as an important alternative to actively screen out.

differential.diagnosis: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / early ARDS post-aspiration
differential.evidence_basis: No relief with positioning (DI-001), clear aspiration precedent (DI-002), no prior cardiac history (DI-003), and profound refractory exhaustion (DI-004) together strongly favour a non-cardiogenic, inflammatory process rather than heart failure.
Lack of positional relief and no prior heart failure history argue strongly against a cardiogenic cause, though formal echocardiogram/BNP would be needed to fully exclude it in hospital.
The clear aspiration precedent and diffuse (rather than focal, pleuritic) presentation make PE less likely as the primary process, though it cannot be fully excluded via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema/ARDS should be leading and the AI Doctor should be directing immediate intensive-care-level emergency transport rather than heart-failure-directed advice.

differential.diagnosis: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, suspected early ARDS post-aspiration
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires chest radiograph, arterial blood gas, JVP/cardiac assessment, and BNP, unavailable remotely. Emergency escalation with advanced airway/ventilation capability is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A management plan that applies standard heart-failure-directed advice (e.g., diuretics, upright positioning as definitive treatment) rather than recognising this as a distinct, non-cardiogenic emergency represents a significant reasoning failure.

red_flags.description: Recent significant witnessed choking/aspiration event approximately 36 hours prior to symptom onset
red_flags.clinical_significance: Establishes the precipitating cause for non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema/ARDS via aspiration-induced diffuse alveolar damage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Aetiology misattributed to a cardiac cause; incorrect (diuretic-focused) management pathway pursued.

red_flags.description: Profound exhaustion and mental sluggishness with severely limited verbal output
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marker of severe hypoxaemia approaching a threshold requiring advanced airway management; obtainable via the pattern and brevity of the patient's responses without measuring vital signs directly.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Impending respiratory failure not recognised; patient deteriorates to respiratory arrest before help arrives.

red_flags.description: SpO2 84% on patient's wearable/pulse oximeter despite a well-ventilated room
red_flags.clinical_significance: Profound, refractory hypoxaemia unresponsive to simple measures, supporting the need for advanced ventilatory support.

red_flags.description: Normal/flat JVP, normal cardiac auscultation without S3, no peripheral oedema on examination (clinician-only findings arguing against a cardiac cause)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms the absence of a cardiac cause, supporting the ARDS/non-cardiogenic diagnosis; requires examination unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — reliance on the historical absence of cardiac history and lack of positional relief substitutes for this examination-based exclusion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Defaulting to heart-failure-directed advice for any severe dyspnoea in an older patient
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor might apply standard cardiogenic-pulmonary-oedema advice (e.g. suggesting upright positioning will help, or framing diuretics as the mainstay) without recognising that the lack of positional relief and clear aspiration precedent point to a fundamentally different, non-cardiogenic process requiring different (ventilatory support, ICU-level) emergency care.

Continuing an extended history-taking exchange with a patient who is profoundly exhausted and mentally sluggish from hypoxaemia risks further exacerbating respiratory fatigue; the AI Doctor should minimise required verbal exchange and escalate rapidly once the key red flags are identified.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Auscultation for diffuse crackles and absence of S3
Arterial blood gas and chest radiograph (bilateral patchy infiltrates)
Formal vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Emergency ambulance (000), Priority 1 dispatch with Intensive Care Paramedic capability given the high likelihood of requiring pre-hospital advanced airway management
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Applying standard heart-failure management advice, or any delay awaiting further clarification from an exhausted patient, risks progression to fatal hypoxaemic respiratory failure.
